summary: German philosopher who wrote Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Beyond Good and Evil, On the Genealogy of Morals, The Birth of Tragedy, and The Gay Science. Known for the Ubermensch, eternal recurrence, amor fati, the will to power, the death of God, master-slave morality, the Apollonian-Dionysian dichotomy, and the concept of ressentiment.

works.name: On the Genealogy of Morals and Master-Slave Morality
works.description: On the Genealogy of Morals is arguably Nietzsche's most analytically important work. It traces a 'revolt of the slaves' in which ressentiment inverted noble values, relabeling nobility as evil. The first essay contrasts master morality (the 'pathos of distance,' giving names) with slave morality (based on ressentiment). The second essay traces the internalization of debt into guilt. The third essay asks 'what do ascetic ideals mean?' and describes the 'ascetic priest' cultivating ressentiment among his 'sick sheep.' The work attacks Paul Ree as an 'English psychologist' and inspired both Bernard Williams's genealogical method and Foucault's 'genealogical method.' Brian Leiter wrote Nietzsche on Morality defending 'methodological naturalism.' The example of lambs complaining about birds of prey illustrates how morality separates strength from its expression.

clues.clue: Master-slave morality: 'pathos of distance' grants right of giving names; slave morality from ressentiment
examples: described a form of this concept characterized by the 'pathos of distance' that grants the 'right of giving names'
describes a revolt caused by ressentiment leading to the rise of this concept's 'slave' form

clues.clue: Three essays: (1) noble vs. slave, (2) debt -> guilt, (3) ascetic ideals; 'sick sheep' and the ascetic priest
examples: traces how ascetic interpretations of suffering as punishment and the internalization of debt into guilt
uses the example of a priest telling 'sick sheep' that they themselves are to blame

clues.clue: Lambs complaining about birds of prey: morality separates strength from its expression
examples: separates strength from its expression according to a passage imagining lambs complaining about birds of prey

examples: contrasts Stendhal's claim that beauty is 'a promise of happiness' with Schopenhauerian aesthetic contemplation

examples: turns to the etymology of words like 'schlecht' and 'schlicht'

titles a posthumous collection of notes which was stitched together and made more anti-Semitic

works.name: The Birth of Tragedy and Apollonian-Dionysian
works.description: The Birth of Tragedy (1872) is Nietzsche's first published book. It contrasts the Apollonian (order, form, differentiation) with the Dionysian (chaos, ecstasy, undifferentiation) as two 'artistic impulses.' The Greeks achieved the highest art through this dichotomy. Tragedy's downfall was caused by Socratic rationalism. Midas is told by Silenus that the best thing for man is never to be born. The work promoted Wagner's operas. Nietzsche praised Aeschylus and Sophocles for combining the two impulses, and condemned Euripides for reducing the chorus and eliminating music.

clues.clue: Apollonian (order) vs. Dionysian (chaos) as two artistic impulses; Greeks achieved highest art through their combination
examples: praised Aeschylus and Sophocles for effectively combining the orgiastic, chaotic side of humanity with the orderly one
Views of reality as differentiated and undifferentiated by forms are the two 'artistic impulses'

clues.clue: Tragedy's downfall caused by Socratic rationalism; Euripides reduced the chorus
examples: this art form's downfall was caused by its infusion with Socratic rationalism
condemns Euripides for reducing the role of the chorus and eliminating music

clues.clue: Silenus tells Midas the best thing for man is never to be born
examples: Midas is told that the best thing for man is to never be born by Silenus

clues.clue: Promoted Wagner's operas (1872 work)
examples: an 1872 book that promoted Wagner's operas

clues.clue: Camille Paglia draws on Apollonian/Dionysian in Sexual Personae ('Sky Cult' vs. 'Earth Cult')
examples: Camille Paglia draws on these concepts to portray Western culture as a struggle between a 'Sky Cult' and an 'Earth Cult'

clues.clue: Schopenhauer's will/representation inspired the dichotomy
examples: they relate closely to the 'will' and 'representation' respectively from this man's ideas

works.name: Eternal Recurrence and Amor Fati
works.description: Eternal recurrence (or eternal return) is the idea that the universe keeps reforming itself. It is introduced by a demon 'stealing into your loneliest existence' in The Gay Science, where it is called 'the most burdensome thought.' Alfred Baeumler (the Nazi interpreter) elided it; Maurice Blanchot called it a 'local vertigo'; Heidegger called it 'the supreme triumph of metaphysics.' Amor fati ('love of fate') is Nietzsche's formula for human greatness and the antidote to eternal recurrence's nihilism; it means embracing anything that occurs as good or necessary. Kundera's Unbearable Lightness of Being opens by naming Nietzsche.

clues.clue: Eternal recurrence: universe keeps reforming; demon 'steals into your loneliest existence'; 'most burdensome thought'
examples: scholars are divided over cosmological and theoretical interpretations of this thinker's 'eternal recurrence of the same'
A demon that 'steals into your loneliest existence' is used to introduce

clues.clue: Amor fati: 'formula for greatness'; embrace fate; antidote to eternal recurrence's nihilism
examples: embracing amor fati would allow humans to accept 'eternal recurrence'
Nietzsche defines this concept as the formula for human greatness

clues.clue: Baeumler (Nazi interpreter) elided eternal recurrence; Blanchot: 'local vertigo'; Heidegger: 'supreme triumph of metaphysics'
examples: Alfred Baeumler elided this concept, since it was not compatible with the 'principle of highest justice'
Martin Heidegger argues that it is the 'supreme triumph of metaphysics'

clues.clue: Kundera's Unbearable Lightness of Being opens by naming Nietzsche
examples: The first sentence of The Unbearable Lightness of Being mentions this philosopher by name

works.name: Thus Spoke Zarathustra and the Ubermensch
works.description: Thus Spoke Zarathustra is a philosophical novel about an Iranian prophet living in a cave with a snake and an eagle. It contains Dionysian hymns (dithyrambs), the concept of the Ubermensch (superman/overman), and the will to power. Zarathustra dreams of a child with a mirror. The first English translation by Alexander Tille predated Thomas Common's controversial 'Superman' rendering by 13 years. Walter Kaufmann translated untergehen as 'go under.' Richard Strauss wrote a famous tone poem inspired by it, with the 'C, G, C' motif used in 2001: A Space Odyssey.

clues.clue: Philosophical novel: Iranian prophet in a cave with snake and eagle; dithyrambs; Ubermensch and will to power
examples: a man who encounters an eagle and a snake describes the process one undergoes in order to become a master of himself
This thinker's sister Elizabeth posthumously published the works of this philosopher

clues.clue: Ubermensch: first translated by Tille as 'Beyond-man'; Common's 'Superman' controversial; Kaufmann critiqued Common
examples: The first English translation of a concept posited by this thinker was made by Alexander Tille 13 years before a controversial rendering by Thomas Common

clues.clue: Richard Strauss tone poem: 'C, G, C' motif used in 2001: A Space Odyssey
examples: A Richard Strauss tone poem inspired by a work by one of these people features a famous rising 'C, G, C' motif

clues.clue: Zarathustra dreams of a child coming with a mirror
examples: A figure postulated by this thinker dreams of a child coming to him with a mirror

works.name: Beyond Good and Evil
works.description: Beyond Good and Evil (subtitled 'Prelude to a Philosophy of the Future') opens with 'Suppose truth is a woman - what then?' It contains the chapter 'The Natural History of Morals,' calls Kant 'the great Chinaman of Konigsberg,' describes 'good Europeans,' and discusses the 'pathos of distance.' Nietzsche uses perspectivism (from Teichmuller) and argues the 'noble soul has reverence for itself.' Famous aphorisms include 'he who fights with monsters' and 'when you gaze into an abyss.' The section 'Maxims and Arrows' was expanded in Twilight of the Idols.

clues.clue: 'Suppose truth is a woman - what then?' opening; 'Prelude to a Philosophy of the Future'
examples: The question, 'Suppose truth is a woman - what then?' begins a book by this thinker

clues.clue: Calls Kant 'the great Chinaman of Konigsberg'; compares him to 'sleepy faculty' of opium
examples: calls Kant the 'great Chinaman of Konigsberg,' and compares him to the 'sleepy faculty' of opium

clues.clue: 'Good Europeans' are 'neither Jesuits, nor democrats, nor even sufficiently Germans'
examples: People who 'are neither Jesuits, nor democrats, nor even sufficiently Germans' are called this kind of 'Europeans'

clues.clue: 'He who fights with monsters' and 'when you gaze long into an abyss'
examples: he who fights with monsters should look to it that he himself does not become a monster
when you gaze long into an abyss the abyss also gazes into you

clues.clue: Pathos of distance grants nobles the right to create values
examples: Nietzsche used this term in Beyond Good and Evil in a phrase that denotes the noble's ability to create values via 'distance'

clues.clue: Perspectivism (from Teichmuller) to criticize pure subjectivism
examples: Gustav Teichmuller's theory of perspectivism was adopted by this thinker to criticize pure subjectivism

works.name: Untimely Meditations and Other Works
works.description: The Untimely Meditations include 'Schopenhauer as Educator' (the third), an attack on David Strauss's 'New Faith' (the first), and 'On the Use and Abuse of History for Life' (the second), which distinguishes monumental, antiquarian, and critical approaches to history. The Gay Science declares 'God is dead' via the parable of the madman with a lantern. Twilight of the Idols has 'Four Great Errors,' 'The Problem of Socrates,' 'Morality as the Enemy of Nature,' and the 'Maxims and Arrows' section. Ecce Homo has chapters like 'Why I Am So Clever' and 'Why I Am a Destiny/Fatality,' ending with Voltaire's 'ecrasez l'infame.' Daybreak contains 'thoughts on the prejudices of morality.' Human, All Too Human began Nietzsche's aphoristic style.

clues.clue: Untimely Meditations: Schopenhauer as Educator (3rd), attack on Strauss (1st), Use/Abuse of History (2nd: monumental, antiquarian, critical)
examples: articulated the 'critical,' 'monumental,' and 'antiquarian' approaches to historical study
attacked the materialist 'New Faith' of Strauss in his Untimely Meditations

clues.clue: 'God is dead' declared via madman with lantern in The Gay Science
examples: declared that 'God is dead' in The Gay Science
announced by a madman holding a lantern

clues.clue: Twilight of the Idols: 'Four Great Errors,' 'Problem of Socrates,' 'Morality as Enemy of Nature'; applauds Caesar, Napoleon, Thucydides
examples: listed as the titular 'Four Great Errors'
called Goethe 'the last German whom I respect'

clues.clue: Ecce Homo: 'Why I Am So Clever,' 'Why I Am a Destiny'; published 1888; ends with Voltaire's 'ecrasez l'infame'

examples: noted that 'in abolishing the true world we have also abolished the world of appearance'

works.name: General / Biographical and Reception
works.description: Nietzsche is identified as a German philosopher who went insane (reportedly after seeing a horse whipped in Turin). His sister Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche (who co-founded the Paraguayan colony Nueva Germania) posthumously edited his works to be more anti-Semitic and fascistic; Mazzino Montinari and Giorgio Colli called The Will to Power a 'historical forgery.' Walter Kaufmann rehabilitated his reputation with his book 'Philosopher, Psychologist, Antichrist,' though it has been criticized for its existentialist portrayal. Paul Ricoeur named Nietzsche, Marx, and Freud as 'masters of suspicion.' Gilles Deleuze rehabilitated Nietzsche via 'active' and 'reactive' forces. Derrida used Nietzsche's thinking as the alternative to 'Rousseauist freeplay.' He used the Tschandala concept from the Laws of Manu.

clues.clue: Sister Elisabeth Forster-Nietzsche edited works to be more anti-Semitic/fascistic; Will to Power may be a forgery
examples: His sister Elizabeth Forster posthumously published the works of this philosopher

examples: Daybreak, Human, All Too Human, The Gay Science, and Beyond Good and Evil are replete with unique method of argumentation

comprehensive_summary:
Friedrich Nietzsche is the most frequently clued philosopher in the dataset, with 22 tossups and 30 bonuses. His giveaways include authorship of Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Beyond Good and Evil, and The Gay Science, and the declaration 'God is dead.'

On the Genealogy of Morals is his most analytically rich work. Its three essays trace: (1) master morality (the 'pathos of distance') vs. slave morality (from ressentiment), using the etymology of 'schlecht'/'schlicht' and the lambs-vs-birds-of-prey image; (2) the internalization of debt into guilt; (3) the meaning of ascetic ideals, with the 'ascetic priest' cultivating ressentiment among 'sick sheep.' It attacks Paul Ree and inspired Foucault's genealogical method and Williams's Truth and Truthfulness.

The Birth of Tragedy (1872) contrasts the Apollonian (order) with the Dionysian (chaos), arguing the Greeks achieved the highest art through their combination. Tragedy declined through Socratic rationalism. Silenus tells Midas the best thing is never to be born.

Eternal recurrence (introduced by a demon in The Gay Science) is 'the most burdensome thought.' Amor fati ('love of fate'), 'the formula for human greatness,' is its antidote. Heidegger called eternal recurrence 'the supreme triumph of metaphysics.'

Thus Spoke Zarathustra features an Iranian prophet with a snake and eagle, the Ubermensch, and the will to power. The first English translation (Tille's 'Beyond-man') preceded Thomas Common's controversial 'Superman.'

Beyond Good and Evil opens with 'Suppose truth is a woman,' calls Kant 'the great Chinaman of Konigsberg,' and contains the famous abyss aphorism. The Untimely Meditations include Schopenhauer as Educator, an attack on Strauss, and the Use/Abuse of History (monumental, antiquarian, critical). Twilight of the Idols has the 'Four Great Errors' and 'Problem of Socrates.' Ecce Homo has 'Why I Am a Destiny.'

His sister Elisabeth edited his works to be more fascistic; Montinari/Colli called The Will to Power a forgery. Walter Kaufmann rehabilitated his reputation. Ricoeur named him one of three 'masters of suspicion' with Marx and Freud. Deleuze and Derrida were key French interpreters.
